perturbation.original_text: Each Recipient agrees that, for eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, no person in its organization who receives or has access to the Evaluation Material or has knowledge about the Possible Transaction will, directly or indirectly, solicit, or cause the soliciting of, or assist or encourage others in the soliciting of, the employment or consulting services of any of the officers of a Provider or any of its subsidiaries or employees engaged in research and development for Provider or any of its subsidiaries or any other employee of the Provider or any if its subsidiaries with whom the Recipient has had contact in connection with its evaluation of a Possible Transaction or of whom it has become aware as a result of its receipt of any Evaluation Material, so long as they are employed by the Provider or any of its subsidiaries and for two months thereafter (each, a “ Covered Employee ”).
perturbation.changed_text: Each Recipient agrees that, for eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, no person in its organization who receives or has access to the Evaluation Material or has knowledge about the Possible Transaction will, directly or indirectly, solicit, or cause the soliciting of, or assist or encourage others in the soliciting of, the employment or consulting services of any of the officers of a Provider or any of its subsidiaries or employees engaged in research and development for Provider or any of its subsidiaries or any other employee of the Provider or any if its subsidiaries with whom the Recipient has had contact in connection with its evaluation of a Possible Transaction or of whom it has become aware as a result of its receipt of any Evaluation Material, so long as they are employed by the Provider or any of its subsidiaries and for one week thereafter (each, a “ Covered Employee ”).
perturbation.explanation: Changing the non-solicitation period after employment ends from two months to one week creates a conflict with California law, which may consider such a short period unreasonable and therefore an unlawful restraint of trade.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. A one-week non-solicitation period may be deemed an unreasonable restraint, especially if it hinders a former employee's ability to find new employment or solicit former colleagues, violating the statute's intent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the non-solicitation period after employment from two months to one week, which creates a contradiction. California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a), as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, generally voids contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The Law Explanation suggests the shortened one-week non-solicitation period may be deemed an unreasonable restraint, potentially violating the statute's intent by hindering a former employee's ability to find new employment or solicit former colleagues. The change weakens the protection offered by the original clause, creating a likely violation of California law, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that, for a period of eighteen (18) months from the date of this Agreement (the “ Standstill Period ”), unless specifically invited in writing by the other Party, neither it nor any of its majority-owned subsidiaries will in any manner, directly or indirectly: (a) effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect, or cause or participate in, or in any way intentionally assist any other Person to effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect or participate in: (i) any acquisition of any securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or assets of the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, (ii) any tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination involving the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, (iii) any recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or other extraordinary transaction with respect to the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents to vote any voting securities of the other Party; (b) form, join or in any way participate in a “group” (as defined under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended) with respect to the securities of the other Party; (c) make any public announcement with respect to, or submit an unsolicited proposal for or offer of (with or without conditions), any extraordinary transaction involving the other Party or its securities or assets; (d) otherwise act, alone or in concert with others, to seek to change or influence the control of the management, Board of Directors or policies of the other Party; (e) take any action which might force the other Party to make a public announcement regarding any of the types of matters set forth in (a) above; or (f) enter into any discussions or arrangements with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that, for a period of five (5) years from the date of this Agreement (the “ Standstill Period ”), unless specifically invited in writing by the other Party, neither it nor any of its majority-owned subsidiaries will in any manner, directly or indirectly: (a) effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect, or cause or participate in, or in any way intentionally assist any other Person to effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect or participate in: (i) any acquisition of any securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or assets of the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, (ii) any tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination involving the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, (iii) any recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or other extraordinary transaction with respect to the other Party or any of its subsidiaries, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents to vote any voting securities of the other Party; (b) form, join or in any way participate in a “group” (as defined under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended) with respect to the securities of the other Party; (c) make any public announcement with respect to, or submit an unsolicited proposal for or offer of (with or without conditions), any extraordinary transaction involving the other Party or its securities or assets; (d) otherwise act, alone or in concert with others, to seek to change or influence the control of the management, Board of Directors or policies of the other Party; (e) take any action which might force the other Party to make a public announcement regarding any of the types of matters set forth in (a) above; or (f) enter into any discussions or arrangements with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the standstill period to five years may be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law, depending on the specific circumstances and the nature of the businesses involved.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: While standstill agreements are common, a five-year duration might be viewed as excessively long and therefore an unreasonable restraint on a party's ability to engage in business activities, potentially violating California Business and Professions Code Section 16600.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the standstill period to five years, which, according to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." While standstill agreements are common, extending the duration to five years may violate this law, creating a legal contradiction because it unreasonably restricts a party's ability to conduct business.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall terminate two years after the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall terminate one year after the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the agreement's term to one year could potentially violate the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) if it unduly restricts the protection of trade secrets for a commercially unreasonable duration.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CUTSA doesn't specify a minimum term for confidentiality agreements, setting a short term like one year might be considered insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially if the information has a longer commercial lifespan. This could expose trade secrets prematurely and undermine the Act's purpose of preventing misappropriation.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the agreement term from two years to one year, potentially contradicting CUTSA's aim to protect trade secrets for a reasonable duration. The Law Explanation suggests a one-year term might be insufficient, especially if the trade secret has a longer commercial lifespan. While neither snippet explicitly states a minimum term, the modification introduces a risk of inadequate protection, undermining the purpose of CUTSA, as it could expose trade secrets prematurely.

perturbation.original_text: Each Recipient shall use the Evaluation Material solely for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction and, subject to Section 5 , will not disclose any of the Evaluation Material in any manner whatsoever; provided, however , that any of such information may be disclosed to the Recipient’s Representatives for the purpose of helping the Recipient evaluate a Possible Transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Recipient shall use the Evaluation Material solely for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction and, subject to Section 5 , will not disclose any of the Evaluation Material in any manner whatsoever; provided, however , that any of such information may be disclosed to the Recipient’s Representatives for the purpose of helping the Recipient evaluate a Possible Transaction. Further, any information may be disclosed publicly after 30 days of the agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a clause that any information may be disclosed publicly after 30 days directly contradicts the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement and potentially violates California trade secret law if the evaluation material contains trade secrets.  This is because it would remove the 'reasonable efforts' to protect trade secrets requirement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) requires companies to make reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of their trade secrets. Allowing public disclosure after only 30 days nullifies any reasonable effort to maintain secrecy, potentially resulting in the loss of trade secret protection under California law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause that permits public disclosure of evaluation material after 30 days, which directly contradicts the principle of maintaining trade secret confidentiality as required by the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). The Law Explanation states this change 'nullifies any reasonable effort to maintain secrecy, potentially resulting in the loss of trade secret protection under California law'. While the scraped snippets themselves do not detail the specific requirements for maintaining trade secret protection, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, combined with the nature of the change, strongly indicate that allowing public disclosure after a short period undermines the reasonable efforts needed to protect trade secrets, thus creating a contradiction.
